mcqs.question: What is the primary advantage of virtual memory over physical memory?

options.text: It provides faster access to data
It allows execution of programs larger than physical memory
It eliminates the need for RAM completely
It increases the clock speed of the CPU

mcqs.explanation: Virtual memory enables programs to use more memory than physically available by swapping pages to disk. This allows execution of programs larger than available RAM, which is its primary advantage. It doesn't eliminate RAM or increase CPU speed.

mcqs.question: In demand paging, when is a page brought into memory?

options.text: Before the process starts execution
When a page fault occurs and the referenced page is not in memory
When the process is created
At regular intervals defined by the scheduler

mcqs.explanation: Demand paging follows the principle of loading pages only when needed. A page is brought into memory only when a process references it and a page fault occurs because it's not currently in memory. This lazy loading approach minimizes I/O and memory usage.

mcqs.question: A page fault occurs when:

options.text: The CPU attempts to access a page currently in memory
The referenced page is not present in physical memory
The TLB hit ratio is 100%
The page replacement algorithm selects a victim page

mcqs.explanation: A page fault is a trap raised by hardware when a process accesses a memory location that is not in physical memory (not in any frame). The OS must then bring the required page from secondary storage into a free frame. TLB hits don't cause page faults.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?

options.text: To store recently used pages for quick replacement
To cache recent virtual-to-physical address translations
To hold the page table entries that have been modified
To track which frames contain free pages

mcqs.explanation: TLB is a hardware cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations (page table entries). It speeds up address translation by avoiding the need to access the page table in memory for every memory access. It's a type of associative cache.

mcqs.question: Consider a system with 5 page frames and the following page reference string: 1, 2, 3, 4, 1, 2, 5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Using FIFO page replacement, how many page faults occur?
mcqs.explanation: FIFO: Frames initially empty. 1→PF, 2→PF, 3→PF, 4→PF (4 faults, frames: 1,2,3,4). 1→hit, 2→hit. 5→PF (replaces 1, frames: 5,2,3,4). 1→PF (replaces 2, frames: 5,1,3,4). 2→PF (replaces 3, frames: 5,1,2,4). 3→PF (replaces 4, frames: 5,1,2,3). 4→PF (replaces 5, frames: 4,1,2,3). 5→PF (replaces 1, frames: 4,5,2,3). Total = 11 page faults.

mcqs.question: Which page replacement algorithm may suffer from Belady's anomaly?

options.text: Least Recently Used (LRU)
Optimal page replacement
First In First Out (FIFO)
Clock (Second Chance)

mcqs.explanation: Belady's anomaly is the counterintuitive phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames can lead to more page faults. FIFO exhibits this anomaly because it doesn't consider page usage patterns. LRU and Optimal algorithms don't suffer from this issue.

mcqs.question: In the Optimal page replacement algorithm, which page is selected for replacement?

options.text: The page that has been in memory the longest time
The page that will not be referenced for the longest time in the future
The page with the lowest count of references
The page that was most recently used

mcqs.explanation: The Optimal algorithm (OPT) replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future. It's theoretically optimal but impractical as it requires future knowledge. It serves as a benchmark for evaluating other algorithms.

mcqs.question: What is copy-on-write (COW) used for?

options.text: To speed up disk writes by deferring actual writes
To allow parent and child processes to share memory until one modifies it
To protect the operating system from malicious code
To implement virtual memory in embedded systems

mcqs.explanation: Copy-on-write is an optimization where parent and child processes initially share the same memory pages. A copy is made only when a process tries to modify a shared page. This saves memory and improves fork() performance, commonly used in Unix-like systems.

mcqs.question: A process has 4KB pages and accesses memory at address 0x1234. What is the page number and offset?

options.text: Page number: 0x123, Offset: 0x4
Page number: 0x1, Offset: 0x234
Page number: 0x0, Offset: 0x1234
Page number: 0x12, Offset: 0x34

mcqs.explanation: With 4KB = 2^12 bytes pages, offset uses 12 bits (lower 3 hex digits). Address 0x1234 = 0x1_234 in hex. Page number = 0x1 (upper bits), Offset = 0x234 (lower 3 digits = 12 bits = 4KB page size).

mcqs.question: What is thrashing?

options.text: When the CPU is overworked and slows down
Excessive page fault activity causing low CPU utilization as time is spent on paging
A condition where all pages are pinned in memory
When the disk queue becomes too long

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing occurs when a process spends more time paging (handling page faults) than executing. This happens when processes demand more frames than available, causing constant page evictions and reloads. CPU utilization drops because the system is busy with I/O instead of useful work.

mcqs.question: The working set model defines working set as:

options.text: All pages currently in physical memory
The set of pages a process has referenced in the last k memory references
The minimum pages required to prevent thrashing
Pages that have been modified since being loaded

mcqs.explanation: The working set is the set of pages a process has referenced in the last τ (time) or k (reference) window. It's used to determine how many frames a process needs. If the working set doesn't fit in memory, thrashing occurs. This model helps in locality-based allocation.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid page replacement algorithm?

options.text: Least Recently Used (LRU)
Most Frequently Used (MFU)
First In First Out (FIFO)
Optimal

mcqs.explanation: MFU (Most Frequently Used) is not a standard page replacement algorithm. The common algorithms are: FIFO, LRU, Optimal, Clock (Second Chance), and their variants. MFU would be counterproductive as frequently used pages are likely to be needed again.

mcqs.question: In a two-level page table system, what is the advantage over a single-level page table?

options.text: Faster address translation
Reduced memory overhead for sparse address spaces
Eliminates page faults completely
Simpler hardware implementation

mcqs.explanation: Two-level page tables reduce memory waste for sparse address spaces. A single-level table needs an entry for every virtual page, even if unused. Two-level tables allow sparse allocation - outer page entries only exist for used regions, saving significant memory for large address spaces.

mcqs.question: What happens during a context switch when using virtual memory?

options.text: The entire page table must be copied to new process
The TLB must be flushed to prevent address translation leaks
All frames must be cleared of old process pages
The swap space is reorganized

mcqs.explanation: When switching between processes, the TLB must be flushed (or address space IDs changed) to prevent the new process from reading the old process's memory. Some architectures use ASIDs (Address Space IDs) to tag TLB entries, allowing selective invalidation instead of full flush.

mcqs.question: What is the clean/dirty bit in a page table entry used for?

options.text: To indicate if the page has been accessed recently
To indicate if the page has been modified since being loaded from disk
To indicate if the page is in the TLB
To indicate if the page is valid or invalid

mcqs.explanation: The dirty (modified) bit indicates whether a page has been modified while in memory. This is crucial for page replacement - if dirty, the page must be written back to disk before eviction. Clean pages can simply be overwritten. The reference (accessed) bit tracks recent use.

mcqs.question: In the Clock (Second Chance) page replacement algorithm, what determines if a page gets a second chance?

options.text: The age of the page in memory
The reference bit of the page
The number of times the page has been accessed
Whether the page is in the working set

mcqs.explanation: The Clock algorithm uses a reference (use) bit. Pages with reference bit = 1 get a second chance (bit cleared, moved to end of circular list). Pages with reference bit = 0 are replaced. This approximates LRU without tracking all references, making it practical and efficient.

mcqs.question: What is pre-paging?

options.text: Loading pages before they are explicitly requested
Writing dirty pages to disk proactively
Reserving frames before a process starts
Loading the entire program at startup

mcqs.explanation: Pre-paging brings pages into memory before they're explicitly referenced, based on predictions of future references. It aims to reduce page faults during program execution by anticipating needs (e.g., loading code sections ahead). However, if predictions are wrong, it wastes I/O and memory.

mcqs.question: Which virtual memory concept ensures that a process cannot access memory belonging to another process?

options.text: Address translation
Memory protection bits in page tables
The TLB
Demand paging

mcqs.explanation: Memory protection bits (read/write/execute permissions) in each page table entry prevent unauthorized access. These bits ensure processes can only access pages allocated to them and only in permitted ways. Address translation alone doesn't provide protection - it's the permission bits that enforce isolation.

mcqs.question: The effective access time (EAT) formula for TLB is: EAT = (hit ratio × memory access) + (miss ratio × (2 × memory access)). Why is memory accessed twice on a miss?

options.text: Once to check TLB, once for actual data
Once to read page table entry, once to read the actual data
Once to translate address, once to validate permissions
Once to load from disk, once to access memory

mcqs.explanation: On a TLB miss, the page table must be consulted in memory (first access) to get the physical frame number. Then a second access is needed to read/write the actual data. Some systems use inverted page tables making this even more expensive. TLB hits avoid this overhead entirely.

mcqs.question: For LRU page replacement with 3 frames and reference string 3,2,1,3,4,3,2,1,5, how many page faults occur?
mcqs.explanation: LRU tracks recency. Frames: 3→PF(1), 2→PF(2), 1→PF(3). 3→hit(3,2,1 in memory). 4→PF(4 replaces 2 - least recent was 2). 3→hit(3,1,4). 2→PF(2 replaces 1 - least recent was 1). 1→PF(1 replaces 4 - least recent was 4). 5→PF(5 replaces 3 - least recent was 2). Total = 10 page faults.
